title: Narrativas periféricas

series_peer_works.title: Distinto amanecer: Max Aub en México, 1943-1944
series_peer_works.author: César A. Núñez

narrative:
Short impact summary

Published in 2019 by Peter Lang Verlag, Iliana Olmedo's Narrativas periféricas is a specialized scholarly monograph situated within the established Exiles and Transterrados series. While formal citation uptake remains limited at this stage, the book holds library presence across multiple institutions and is commercially available through major retail channels, indicating a steady if modest footprint in its niche field.

Full narrative

Narrativas periféricas occupies a distinctive position within Peter Lang's Exiles and Transterrados series (ISSN 2297-9263), a dedicated venue for scholarship on Spanish exile, diaspora, and transterrado experience. Olmedo's contribution sits alongside a growing body of peer works in the series, including studies on Max Aub, the poetry of the Republican exile, and philosophical dimensions of displacement — titles by scholars such as César A. Núñez, Bernard Sicot, and Antolín Sánchez Cuervo. This editorial context signals that the book is positioned as part of a sustained, collective scholarly conversation rather than an isolated intervention, and the series continues to expand, with volumes projected through at least 2026.

The book's institutional reach, while early-stage, is confirmed by holdings recorded across at least four libraries in open catalogues, and its presence on WorldCat makes it discoverable to researchers navigating collections in Hispanic studies, history, and cultural memory. Commercial availability through major retail and reader platforms further broadens access beyond strictly academic library circuits, a meaningful consideration for a monograph addressing peripheral or marginalized literary and historical voices.

Measured against broader citation benchmarks, Narrativas periféricas currently sits below the median for works indexed in comparable databases, reflecting a pattern common to specialized humanities monographs — particularly those engaging with Spanish-language scholarship — where citation cycles tend to be longer and less immediately visible in English-language indices. The book's thematic focus on history and peripheral narratives places it within a field where scholarly engagement often manifests through subsequent monographs, edited volumes, and dissertation work rather than journal article citations alone.

Taken together, the evidence suggests a work that has entered the scholarly record with the institutional legitimacy of a peer-reviewed series and a reputable international press, with its longer-term influence yet to be fully measured. As the Exiles and Transterrados series continues to grow in scope and visibility, earlier volumes such as this one may attract renewed attention from researchers tracing the intellectual lineage of the field. Olmedo's contribution represents a grounded addition to a specialized but actively developing area of historical and literary inquiry.
